folio.label: 162v
folio.l: olde wyse men , and bygonnen to make noyse , and seiden , that

rigút so , as whil that Iren is hoot , men shulde smyte , rigút
so men shuln do wreken here wronges , whil that they ben fresshe
& newe , And w loude vois , they cryden , werre , werre ,

vp roos tho ,
oon of theise olde wyse , & witú his hond mad countenau~ce , æ men
shulde holde he~ stille , & ©eue hi~ audience

¢ Lordynges q¶ he , there
is ful many a man that crietú werre , werre , æat wot ful litel
what werre amountetú , 72Cross in dry point in the right margin.ODR

werre at his bigynnyngö hatú so gret
an entryngö , & so large , æ euäy wigút may entre whan hi~ liketú
& ligútly fynde werre ,

But certes to what ende , that shal æer
of bifalle , it is nat ligút to knowe ,

for sotúly , whan æ werre , is
ones bygonne , there is ful many a child vn born~ of his moder
that shal sterue ©ongö , by cause of thilke werre , or elles lyue in
sorwe , & dye in wrecchednesse ,

And therfore , er that any werre
be bigonne , men must haue gret counseil , & gret deliberaciou~ ,

&
wha~ne this olde man wende to enforce his tale by resou~s , wel
ny alle attones bigo~ne they to ryse , for to breken his tale , & beden
hi~ ful ofte , hise wordes for to a bregge ,

for sotúly he that pächetú
to hem , that listen nat heren his wordes , his Sarmou~ , he~ anoy-
etú

¢ For Iú~c Syrak seitú , That Musyk in wepyngö , is anoy-
ous thyng , This is to seyn , as muche auailletú to speken biforn~
folk , to whicú his speche a noyetú , as it is to synge biforn hi~ æ
wepetú 79Cross in dry point in the right margin.ODR

¢ And whan this wys man sey , æ hi~ wanted audience ,
Al shamefast , he sette hi~ doun a geyn ,

for Salamon seitú , æäe
as thow ne maist haue noon audience , enforce the nat to speke ,

I se wel q¶ this wyse man , æ the co~e ¸uäbe is sotú , æ good cou~-
seil wantetú , whan it is most nede

¢ ñet had this Melibeus in
his counseil , many folk , that péuely in his Ere , cou~seilled hi~ cer-
teyn thyng , and cou~seilled hi~ the contrie in genäal audience

¢ wha~
Melibeus had herd , that the grettest ¹tie of his cou~seil , were acor-
ded , that he shulde make werre , a noon he consented to here cou~-
seillynge , & fully affermed here sentence

¢ Tha~ne Dame Pru-
dence , whan that she sey , how æ hire housbonde shop~ hi~ , for to
wreke hi~ oon his foos , and to bigynne werre , she in ful humble
wyse , whan she sey hire tyme , seide hi~ theise wordes

¢ My lord
q¶ she , I ©ow byseche , as hertily as I dar & kan , ne haste ©ow nat
to faste , and for alle gerdou~s , as ©if me audience

¢ For Piers Al-
fonse seitú , who so that dotú to the , outher good or harm , haste
the nat to quyte it , for in this wyse , thy frend wol a bide , &
thyn enemy , shal the lengere lyue in drede

¢ The ¸uäbe seitú
he hastetú wel , that wysly kan a byde , And in wykked hast , is no
¸fyt

¢ This Melibe answered vn to his wyf , Pèdence , I purpo-
se nat q¶ he , to werkyn by thy counseil , for many causes & re-
sons , For certes euäy wigút wold holde me tha~ne a fool ,

this is to
seyn ; If I for thy cou~seillyngö , wolde chaunge thynges , thatMissing folio
